x_posts.text: VRM discovery calls stay at the technical level because personal questions are uncomfortable. That's a choice, and it costs deals.
A lot of owners don't actually want better property management. They want their brain back.
When an owner says communication is bad, that's usually not the real problem. That's just the first problem that's easy to say out loud.
A better discovery question than 'what are you looking for in a manager?' is 'walk me through what managing this property looks like for you right now.' Much harder to hide inside a generic answer.

The conversation changes when the owner stops talking about response times and says something like 'I haven't had a phone-free weekend in three years.' At that point you're not in a feature conversation anymore.

VRM discovery calls stay at the technical level because personal questions are uncomfortable. Are you seeing owners in your market get more direct about the real pain, or does the surface-level conversation still dominate?
Went deeper on this in this week's VRM Sales Library, because owner conversations get a lot more useful once you stop treating the first complaint like the real problem.
Been applying Jim Keenan's Gap Selling to VRM acquisition this week. The operators who stop at the surface complaint sound identical to every other manager the owner has already talked to.

tweets: Most VRM discovery calls go sideways at the same moment.
The owner says something like communication has been bad, and the manager starts solving communication.

Bad communication is the surface problem. The real problem is usually what that failure has been costing the owner in revenue, time, trust, or just the ability to stop thinking about the property for five minutes.
That's the part many people skip because it gets personal, and personal is harder than talking about software and response times.
But that's also where the real close lives.
When the owner says 'I haven't had a weekend off in three years,' you're not in a feature conversation anymore.
Now you're in a relief conversation.
